interviewers.name: Sarah Chen
interviewers.role: Head of Product, Checkout & Payment Links
interviewers.background: Former Engineering Manager at Google. Joined Stripe in 2019. Focuses on reducing friction in the global payment flow.

public_writing.title: The Long Tail of Global Payment Methods
public_writing.summary: Discusses the technical and UX challenges of integrating local payment methods (LPMs) like Pix and iDEAL.

recent_talks.key_point: Conversion is the ultimate metric for merchants; every millisecond of latency is a lost sale.

likely_questions: How would you prioritize adding a new payment method versus improving the reliability of an existing one?
Tell me about a time you had to balance a highly technical constraint with a seamless user experience.

interviewers.name: Marcus Thorne
interviewers.role: Senior Staff Engineer, Billing & Revenue Delivery
interviewers.background: Infrastructure specialist. Passionate about API design and backward compatibility. 8 years at Stripe.

public_writing.title: API Versioning at Scale
public_writing.summary: Deep dive into why Stripe rarely breaks APIs and how to build 'forever' infrastructure.

recent_talks.key_point: A PM's most important job is ensuring the product feels like it was built by one person, not ten teams.

likely_questions: How do you handle a situation where a product requirement threatens the simplicity of our API surface?
Describe a complex system you managed where the migration path was harder than the build.

interviewers.name: Dr. Aris Varma
interviewers.background: PhD in Computational Linguistics. Previously at OpenAI. Focuses on RLHF and model alignment.

public_writing.title: Constitutional AI: A Product Perspective
public_writing.summary: Explaining how safety guardrails can actually improve model utility rather than just being a 'filter'.

recent_talks.key_point: Users don't want 'safe' models that refuse to answer; they want models that understand nuance and intent.

likely_questions: What is the biggest product risk in LLM deployment over the next 24 months?
How do you define 'quality' for a generative output that has no single 'right' answer?

interviewers.name: Elena Rodriguez
interviewers.background: Ex-AWS. Scaling developer ecosystems. Joined Anthropic to lead the API and Workbench initiatives.

public_writing.title: The Prompt Engineering Trap
public_writing.summary: Argues that the goal of a platform should be to make prompt engineering unnecessary through better model defaults.

recent_talks.key_point: The new 'stack' is evolving weekly; PMs must build for flexibility, not just fixed features.

likely_questions: How would you entice developers to switch from OpenAI to Claude for their core production workloads?
Walk me through how you would design an evaluation framework for a new enterprise API feature.

interviewers.name: Karri Saarinen
interviewers.role: CEO & Co-Founder (Interviews for Senior PM roles)
interviewers.background: Former Principal Designer at Airbnb. Created the Airbnb Design System. Obsessed with craft and tool quality.

public_writing.title: The Linear Method
public_writing.summary: Principles of modern software development: focus, momentum, and high-fidelity execution.

recent_talks.key_point: We don't do A/B testing for features; we build based on opinion and taste.

likely_questions: What is a piece of software you find beautiful, and why?
Linear is opinionated software. How do you decide when to listen to a customer versus sticking to our philosophy?

interviewers.name: Nan Yu
interviewers.background: Former CTO at several startups. Focuses on the intersection of engineering velocity and product management.

public_writing.title: Why Your PM Doesn't Need Jira
public_writing.summary: A critique of bloated enterprise tools and a plea for 'un-management'.

recent_talks.key_point: A great PM creates the environment for engineers to ship, rather than managing the shipping themselves.

likely_questions: How do you maintain momentum in a team when a project hits a technical dead end?
Give me an example of a feature you chose *not* to build despite high user demand.
